yaml.title: Exploratory Sales Analysis
yaml.description: Cleaning and exploring a retail dataset to surface seasonal trends and product performance using Pandas and Plotly.
yaml.author: Raunit Budhiraja

markdown.markdown:
Every retail business generates transaction logs — thousands of rows tracking what sold, when, and where. The problem is rarely a lack of data. It's knowing what to look at first.[^1] [^1]: This is sometimes called the "curse of dimensionality" in data science — more data doesn't automatically mean more insight. This project takes a synthetic retail dataset and works through a structured EDA process: cleaning, understanding the shape, then asking progressively sharper questions until a few genuinely useful patterns emerge. --- ## EDA Workflow The process followed here is a standard analytical pipeline: ```{mermaid} flowchart LR A[Load Data] --> B[Quality Check] B --> C[Univariate Analysis] C --> D[Bivariate Analysis] D --> E[Time Series Trends] E --> F[Insights & Conclusions] ``` --- ## Dataset The dataset is a synthetic retail sales log covering 12 months across four product categories. | Column | Type | Description | |---|---|---| | `date` | datetime | Transaction date | | `category` | string | Product category | | `region` | string | Irish region | | `units_sold` | int | Units per transaction | | `revenue` | float | Revenue in euros (€) | --- ## Setup & Data Loading ```{python} #| label: setup #| echo: true import pandas as pd import plotly.express as px import plotly.graph_objects as go import numpy as np np.random.seed(42) dates = pd.date_range("2024-01-01", "2024-12-31", freq="D") categories = ["Electronics", "Apparel", "Home & Garden", "Sports"] regions = ["Dublin", "Cork", "Galway", "Limerick"] rows = [] for date in dates: for _ in range(np.random.randint(3, 8)): cat = np.random.choice(categories) base = {"Electronics": 180, "Apparel": 55, "Home & Garden": 90, "Sports": 70}[cat] seasonal = 1 + 0.3 * np.sin((date.month - 1) * np.pi / 6) rows.append({ "date": date, "category": cat, "region": np.random.choice(regions), "units_sold": int(np.random.poisson(seasonal * 5)), "revenue": round(base * seasonal * np.random.uniform(0.85, 1.15) * np.random.poisson(seasonal * 5), 2) }) df = pd.DataFrame(rows) df["month"] = df["date"].dt.to_period("M").astype(str) df["month_num"] = df["date"].dt.month print(f"Rows: {len(df):,} | Columns: {df.shape[1]}") df.head() ``` --- ## Data Quality Check ```{python} #| label: quality-check print("Null values:\n", df.isnull().sum()) print(f"\nDate range: {df['date'].min().date()} → {df['date'].max().date()}") print(f"Categories: {df['category'].unique()}") ``` No nulls, correct types, full year of coverage. --- ## Revenue Formula The synthetic revenue for each transaction is modelled as: $$R = B \cdot S \cdot \epsilon \cdot U$$ Where: - $B$ = base price per category - $S$ = seasonal multiplier $\left(1 + 0.3\sin\left(\frac{(m-1)\pi}{6}\right)\right)$ - $\epsilon$ = random noise $\sim \text{Uniform}(0.85, 1.15)$ - $U$ = units sold $\sim \text{Poisson}(5S)$ --- ## Monthly Revenue Trend ```{python} #| label: monthly-trend #| fig-cap: "Monthly revenue across the full year, showing a clear seasonal peak in Q4." monthly = df.groupby("month")["revenue"].sum().reset_index() monthly.columns = ["month", "total_revenue"] fig = px.line( monthly, x="month", y="total_revenue", title="Monthly Revenue — Full Year", labels={"month": "", "total_revenue": "Revenue (€)"}, markers=True, color_discrete_sequence=["#7fff6e"] ) fig.update_layout( plot_bgcolor="#14141f", paper_bgcolor="#14141f", font_color="#9898b0", title_font_color="#ffffff", xaxis=dict(gridcolor="#252535", tickangle=-30), yaxis=dict(gridcolor="#252535"), ) fig.update_traces(line_width=2.5) fig.show() ``` --- ## Revenue by Category ```{python} #| label: category-revenue #| fig-cap: "Total annual revenue by product category. Electronics leads by a significant margin." cat_rev = df.groupby("category")["revenue"].sum().reset_index().sort_values("revenue", ascending=True) fig = px.bar( cat_rev, y="category", x="revenue", orientation="h", title="Total Revenue by Category", labels={"category": "", "revenue": "Revenue (€)"}, color="revenue", color_continuous_scale=["#252535", "#7fff6e"] ) fig.update_layout( plot_bgcolor="#14141f", paper_bgcolor="#14141f", font_color="#9898b0", title_font_color="#ffffff", xaxis=dict(gridcolor="#252535"), coloraxis_showscale=False ) fig.show() ``` --- ## Regional Breakdown ```{python} #| label: regional #| fig-cap: "Revenue split by region and category. Distribution is broadly even across Irish regions." region_cat = df.groupby(["region", "category"])["revenue"].sum().reset_index() fig = px.bar( region_cat, x="region", y="revenue", color="category", title="Revenue by Region and Category", labels={"region": "", "revenue": "Revenue (€)", "category": "Category"}, barmode="group", color_discrete_sequence=["#7fff6e", "#68d9ff", "#a78bfa", "#ffd166"] ) fig.update_layout( plot_bgcolor="#14141f", paper_bgcolor="#14141f", font_color="#9898b0", title_font_color="#ffffff", yaxis=dict(gridcolor="#252535"), legend=dict(bgcolor="rgba(0,0,0,0)") ) fig.show() ``` --- ## Project Checklist Progress through this analysis: - [x] Load and inspect the dataset - [x] Check for nulls and data type issues - [x] Analyse monthly revenue trend - [x] Break down revenue by category - [x] Explore regional distribution - [ ] Build a predictive sales forecast (future work) --- ## Key Takeaways ::: {.callout-note} ## What this analysis found - Revenue follows a clear **seasonal arc** peaking in Q4 — promotional spend should be front-loaded into Q3 to capture the ramp-up. - **Electronics** generates disproportionate revenue relative to transaction count — protecting margin here matters more than volume discounts. - **Regional revenue is broadly distributed**, so a Dublin-only marketing focus would miss a substantial share of the market. ::: ::: {.callout-tip} ## Next steps A natural follow-up is forecasting: does the Q4 peak come from higher basket sizes, or more transactions? That's the next query to write. ::: --- ## Reflection This project reinforced something I keep running into — the most useful output of an EDA isn't the visualisations themselves, it's the shortlist of questions they raise. Clean data still takes time, even when it's synthetic. There were real decisions to make about aggregation level, date formatting, and how to handle multi-category breakdowns without obscuring the signal.
[← Back to Projects](../index.qmd){.btn-ghost}

Distribution is broadly even across Irish regions." region_cat = df.groupby(["region", "category"])["revenue"].sum().reset_index() fig = px.bar( region_cat, x="region", y="revenue", color="category", title="Revenue by Region and Category", labels={"region": "", "revenue": "Revenue (€)", "category": "Category"}, barmode="group", color_discrete_sequence=["#7fff6e", "#68d9ff", "#a78bfa", "#ffd166"] ) fig.update_layout( plot_bgcolor="#14141f", paper_bgcolor="#14141f", font_color="#9898b0", title_font_color="#ffffff", yaxis=dict(gridcolor="#252535"), legend=dict(bgcolor="rgba(0,0,0,0)") ) fig.show() ``` --- ## Project Checklist Progress through this analysis: - [x] Load and inspect the dataset - [x] Check for nulls and data type issues - [x] Analyse monthly revenue trend - [x] Break down revenue by category - [x] Explore regional distribution - [ ] Build a predictive sales forecast (future work) --- ## Key Takeaways ::: {.callout-note} ## What this analysis found - Revenue follows a clear **seasonal arc** peaking in Q4 — promotional spend should be front-loaded into Q3 to capture the ramp-up. - **Electronics** generates disproportionate revenue relative to transaction count — protecting margin here matters more than volume discounts. - **Regional revenue is broadly distributed**, so a Dublin-only marketing focus would miss a substantial share of the market. ::: ::: {.callout-tip} ## Next steps A natural follow-up is forecasting: does the Q4 peak come from higher basket sizes, or more transactions?
